data.name: VdotCard_Title
data.value: Your VDOT

data.name: VdotCard_Description
data.value: Great! From your reference race we can determine your individual VDOT. This forms the basis for your personal training zones.

data.name: RaceCard_Result
data.value: Result:

data.name: RaceCard_Edit
data.value: Edit race

data.name: PaceInfo_MoreInfo
data.value: More info

data.name: PaceInfo_Free_Title
data.value: Free Pace

data.name: PaceInfo_Free_Value
data.value: No fixed target pace

data.name: PaceInfo_Free_Description
data.value: Choose the pace freely. Use this zone for relaxed movement, drills, warm-ups, or runs where effort matters more than a target split.

data.name: PaceInfo_Recovery_Title
data.value: Recovery Pace

data.name: PaceInfo_Recovery_Description
data.value: Your deliberately relaxed recovery pace. Stay clearly below easy effort so your next quality session stays fresh.

data.name: PaceInfo_EPace_Title
data.value: Easy Pace (E-Pace)

data.name: PaceInfo_EPace_Description
data.value: Your easy run pace. Run at this pace for recovery or easy training. It builds aerobic endurance and aids recovery.

data.name: PaceInfo_MPace_Title
data.value: Marathon Pace (M-Pace)

data.name: PaceInfo_MPace_Description
data.value: Your marathon pace. Training in this zone improves your ability to maintain a steady pace over long distances.

data.name: PaceInfo_TPace_Title
data.value: Threshold Pace (T-Pace)

data.name: PaceInfo_TPace_Description
data.value: Your threshold pace. Training in this zone improves your lactate threshold and helps you run faster without accumulating too much lactic acid.

data.name: PaceInfo_IPace_Title
data.value: Interval Pace (I-Pace)

data.name: PaceInfo_IPace_Description
data.value: Your interval pace. Short, intense efforts in this zone improve your maximum oxygen uptake (VO2max).

data.name: PaceInfo_RPace_Title
data.value: Repetition Pace (R-Pace)

data.name: PaceInfo_RPace_Description
data.value: Your repetition pace. Very short, fast runs at this pace improve your running economy and speed.

data.name: AddRaceDialog_Title
data.value: New Race

data.name: AddRaceDialog_Name
data.value: Name

data.name: AddRaceDialog_Distance
data.value: Distance

data.name: AddRaceDialog_Time
data.value: Race time

data.name: AddRaceDialog_TimeHelper
data.value: hh:mm:ss

data.name: AddRaceDialog_Date
data.value: Date

data.name: AddRaceDialog_Warning_Title
data.value: Attention

data.name: AddRaceDialog_Warning_Message
data.value: Please check that the race time is in the correct format (hh:mm:ss).

data.name: AddRaceDialog_Ok
data.value: Ok

data.name: PaceComparison_Zone
data.value: Zone

data.name: PaceComparison_Daniels
data.value: Daniels

data.name: PaceComparison_CriticalSpeed
data.value: Critical Speed

data.name: PaceModelInfo_Rationale_Title
data.value: Why pace-guided training?

data.name: PaceModelInfo_Rationale_Text
data.value: We primarily control training zones through pace because running speed is a fairly good proxy for external biomechanical load. The core issue is the different adaptation speed of the systems involved: beginners often feel cardiovascularly fitter after only a few weeks, while tendons, bones, joints, and muscle-tendon structures need to adapt much more gradually to repeated impact loading. If training is guided only by heart rate or generic zone systems, this mechanical exposure is easily underestimated. Pace provides a stable external target for how much forceful running is repeated; heart rate remains useful as a complementary signal for internal strain and recovery. Our working hypothesis is: heart-rate-guided training is very useful for athletes who have trained consistently for years, whose musculoskeletal system is already resilient, and whose further adaptation needs very specific stimuli for circulation, metabolism, and muscle work. In the first years of training, however, it often places too much emphasis on cardiovascular effort instead of mechanical load progression and can be harmful over the long term. For heterogeneous group training, the model should also be simple, equipment-light, and consistent over several months. It should accept different inputs, support race predictions, and map a manageable set of training zones in practice. Pace is the simplest common language for this: measurable, comparable, and immediately usable in training.

data.name: PaceModelInfo_Rationale_PaceProxy_Text
data.value: We primarily control training zones through pace because running speed is a fairly good proxy for external biomechanical load.

data.name: PaceModelInfo_Rationale_Adaptation_Text
data.value: The core issue is the different adaptation speed of the systems involved: beginners often feel cardiovascularly fitter after only a few weeks, while tendons, bones, joints, and muscle-tendon structures need to adapt much more gradually to repeated impact loading.

data.name: PaceModelInfo_Rationale_Remaining_Text
data.value: If training is guided only by heart rate or generic zone systems, this mechanical exposure is easily underestimated. Pace provides a stable external target for how much forceful running is repeated; heart rate remains useful as a complementary signal for internal strain and recovery. Our working hypothesis is: heart-rate-guided training is very useful for athletes who have trained consistently for years, whose musculoskeletal system is already resilient, and whose further adaptation needs very specific stimuli for circulation, metabolism, and muscle work. In the first years of training, however, it often places too much emphasis on cardiovascular effort instead of mechanical load progression and can be harmful over the long term. For heterogeneous group training, the model should also be simple, equipment-light, and consistent over several months. It should accept different inputs, support race predictions, and map a manageable set of training zones in practice. Pace is the simplest common language for this: measurable, comparable, and immediately usable in training.

data.name: PaceModelInfo_AdaptationDiagramAlt
data.value: Conceptual diagram comparing adaptation timelines of the nervous system, cardiovascular system, skeletal muscle, tendon, and bone.

data.name: PaceModelInfo_Daniels_Title
data.value: Daniels/VDOT

data.name: PaceModelInfo_Daniels_Text
data.value: The Daniels/VDOT model originates in the work of Jack Daniels and Jimmy Gilbert, published in 1979 as Oxygen Power: Performance Tables for Distance Runners. VDOT is not a directly measured VO2max, but an effective or pseudo-VO2max inferred from actual running performance. A race result is translated into one performance value through running velocity, the oxygen cost of running, and the fraction of VO2max that can be sustained for the race duration. The model therefore combines aerobic capacity, running economy, and race execution in a practical index. It models equivalent performances and training paces best when the input is recent, maximal, and representative of the athlete's profile. Its accuracy is weaker for unusual specialization, very short tests, heat, altitude, trail profiles, or poorly paced efforts. Its main strength is coaching consistency: a known VDOT maps to standardized zones such as Easy, Marathon, Threshold, Interval, and Repetition.

data.name: PaceModelInfo_Cs_Title
data.value: Critical Speed

data.name: PaceModelInfo_Cs_Text
data.value: Critical Speed (CS) is the running-specific form of the critical-power concept. It describes the asymptotic speed of the distance-time relationship and is physiologically interpreted as the boundary between heavy and severe intensity: below CS, a metabolic steady state is more likely; above CS, fatigue-related markers rise progressively until the effort must stop or speed must be reduced. The finite distance capacity above CS is modelled as D'. Anderson et al. (2026) summarize measurement and modelling methods for CS and D' in running and regard CS as a validated boundary between intensity domains, while noting protocol dependence and open questions about D' reconstitution. Lipkova et al. (2025) show in a systematic review of field testing that time trials and the 3-minute all-out test are practical methods for runners when testing conditions and analysis are standardized.

data.name: PaceModelInfo_Calculation_Title
data.value: Conversion in PaceLetics

data.name: PaceModelInfo_Calculation_Text
data.value: All calculations are performed internally with speed, because percentages on pace would be inverted. For Daniels, the entered performance is matched against the VDOT table; the nearest table value returns the classic pace table for Recovery, Easy, Marathon, Threshold, Interval, and Repetition. CS is no longer interpreted as a second Daniels table. From the running data we estimate Critical Speed and D': a single 1200 m test estimates CS conservatively as 0.84 * v1200, and a single 3 km test as 0.90 * v3k. If 1200 m and 3600 m are available, we calculate CS = (3600 - 1200) / (t3600 - t1200) and D' = 1200 - CS * t1200; with several distances we use regression. The CS system then controls intervals by budget: below CS no D' is used; above CS, the planned D' share per repetition determines target speed. Long intervals from 800 to 1600 m use smaller budgets with half recovery, while fast 200 and 400 m intervals use higher budgets with long recovery.

data.name: PaceModelInfo_References_Title
data.value: References

data.name: PaceModelInfo_Anderson_Title
data.value: Anderson et al. (2026): The Measurement and Application of Critical Speed and D' in Running.

data.name: PaceModelInfo_Anderson_Text
data.value: This work reviews measurement protocols, modelling approaches, performance relevance, and training applications of CS and D' in running.

data.name: PaceModelInfo_Lipkova_Title
data.value: Lipkova et al. (2025): Field-based tests for determining critical speed among runners and its practical application.

data.name: PaceModelInfo_Lipkova_Text
data.value: This systematic review evaluates field-based CS tests for runners and highlights time trials and 3MT as practical methods.

data.name: PaceModelInfo_Hawley_Title
data.value: Hawley et al. (2018): Adaptations to Endurance and Strength Training.

data.name: PaceModelInfo_Hawley_Text
data.value: The review summarizes endurance and strength adaptations and includes the classic pattern of early neural strength gains followed by slower muscle mass changes.

data.name: PaceModelInfo_MacInnis_Title
data.value: MacInnis & Gibala (2017): Physiological adaptations to interval training and the role of exercise intensity.

data.name: PaceModelInfo_MacInnis_Text
data.value: This review describes aerobic and skeletal-muscle remodelling from endurance and interval training, including changes in VO2max and mitochondrial capacity.

data.name: PaceModelInfo_Kubo_Title
data.value: Kubo et al. (2010): Time course of changes in muscle and tendon properties during strength training and detraining.

data.name: PaceModelInfo_Kubo_Text
data.value: The study shows that muscle function and neural activation adapt earlier than muscle morphology and tendon stiffness, creating a temporary mismatch.

data.name: PaceModelInfo_Bohm_Title
data.value: Bohm et al. (2019): Functional Adaptation of Connective Tissue by Training.

data.name: PaceModelInfo_Bohm_Text
data.value: The review explains tendon-specific adaptation, slower tendon response rates, and periods of imbalance between muscle and tendon capacities.

data.name: PaceModelInfo_Bohm2015_Title
data.value: Bohm et al. (2015): Human tendon adaptation in response to mechanical loading.

data.name: PaceModelInfo_Bohm2015_Text
data.value: The meta-analysis shows that tendons respond to mechanical loading; loading intensity and longer interventions especially improve stiffness and material properties.

data.name: PaceModelInfo_Papagiannaki_Title
data.value: Papagiannaki et al. (2020): Running-Related Injury From an Engineering, Medical and Sport Science Perspective.

data.name: PaceModelInfo_Papagiannaki_Text
data.value: This mini-review frames running injuries through repetitive impact mechanics, tissue loading, and training-related stress-frequency problems.

data.name: PaceModelInfo_Jiang_Title
data.value: Jiang et al. (2024): Comparison of ground reaction forces as running speed increases between male and female runners.

data.name: PaceModelInfo_Jiang_Text
data.value: This study measured GRF variables at seven running speeds and found significant speed-related changes and correlations.

data.name: PaceModelInfo_Billat_Title
data.value: Billat et al. (2020): Pacing Strategy Affects the Sub-Elite Marathoner's Cardiac Drift and Performance.

data.name: PaceModelInfo_Billat_Text
data.value: The study links pacing, cardiac drift, speed decline, and marathon performance in real race data.

data.name: PaceModelInfo_Warden_Title
data.value: Warden et al. (2021): Preventing Bone Stress Injuries in Runners with Optimal Workload.

data.name: PaceModelInfo_Warden_Text
data.value: The review frames bone stress injury risk as a workload problem where repeated loading cycles can exceed bone tissue capacity.

data.name: PaceModelInfo_DiagramAlt
data.value: Diagram of a workload curve with Daniels/VDOT pace zones and the Critical Speed boundary.

data.name: CriticalSpeedBudget_Title
data.value: CS budget model

data.name: CriticalSpeedBudget_Subtitle
data.value: Intervals are calculated from Critical Speed and the available D' budget per repetition.

data.name: CriticalSpeedBudget_CriticalSpeed
data.value: Critical Speed

data.name: CriticalSpeedBudget_DPrime
data.value: D' budget

data.name: CriticalSpeedBudget_NoDPrime
data.value: D' use: 0 percent

data.name: CriticalSpeedBudget_TableReference
data.value: See interval suggestions below

data.name: CriticalSpeedBudget_Recovery_Title
data.value: Recovery

data.name: CriticalSpeedBudget_Recovery_Description
data.value: Very relaxed running clearly below CS. This zone should stay mechanically light and use no D' budget.

data.name: CriticalSpeedBudget_Easy_Title
data.value: Easy

data.name: CriticalSpeedBudget_Easy_Description
data.value: Relaxed aerobic running below Critical Speed. The pace remains controlled and budget-neutral.

data.name: CriticalSpeedBudget_Threshold_Title
data.value: Threshold

data.name: CriticalSpeedBudget_Threshold_Description
data.value: Running just below CS. This sits at the stable boundary without deliberately emptying the finite capacity above CS.

data.name: CriticalSpeedBudget_LongZone_Title
data.value: Interval

data.name: CriticalSpeedBudget_LongZone_Description
data.value: 800 to 1600 m above CS with half recovery. Pace is derived from a limited D' share per repetition.

data.name: CriticalSpeedBudget_LongZone_Budget
data.value: D' target: about 22 to 42 percent per repetition

data.name: CriticalSpeedBudget_FastZone_Title
data.value: Fast Interval

data.name: CriticalSpeedBudget_FastZone_Description
data.value: 200 to 400 m fast with long recovery. Each repetition may use more D' so quality stays high.

data.name: CriticalSpeedBudget_FastZone_Budget
data.value: D' target: about 8 to 12 percent per repetition

data.name: CriticalSpeedBudget_LongIntervals_Title
data.value: Long intervals

data.name: CriticalSpeedBudget_LongIntervals_Subtitle
data.value: 800, 1000, 1200, and 1600 m with about half recovery.

data.name: CriticalSpeedBudget_FastIntervals_Title
data.value: Fast short intervals

data.name: CriticalSpeedBudget_FastIntervals_Subtitle
data.value: 200 and 400 m with long recovery so the D' budget can mostly reconstitute between repetitions.

data.name: CriticalSpeedBudget_Work
data.value: Work

data.name: CriticalSpeedBudget_Recovery
data.value: Recovery

data.name: PaceModelInfo_MechanicalLoadDiagramAlt
data.value: Diagram showing the relationship between running pace and normalized ground reaction force variables based on Jiang et al. 2024.

data.name: PaceModelInfo_LoadAdaptation_Title
data.value: Is high pace inherently bad?

data.name: PaceModelInfo_LoadAdaptation_Text
data.value: No. Mechanical load is not only a risk; it is also required for adaptation. Tendons and bone respond to sufficiently strong loading stimuli: Bohm et al. (2015) show in a meta-analysis that tendon stiffness and material properties improve especially with higher loading intensities. The diagram below uses their reported effect sizes: high intensities above 70 percent MVC/RM produced a clear tendon-stiffness adaptation, while low intensities were near zero. Warden et al. (2021) frame bone stress injuries not as a consequence of load itself, but as a workload dosing error. For training, this means we do not avoid fast paces; we dose them.

data.name: PaceModelInfo_LoadAdaptation_Tendon_Text
data.value: No. Mechanical load is not only a risk; it is also required for adaptation. Tendons and bone respond to sufficiently strong loading stimuli: Bohm et al. (2015) show in a meta-analysis that tendon stiffness and material properties improve especially with higher loading intensities.

data.name: PaceModelInfo_LoadAdaptation_Diagram_Text
data.value: The diagram below uses their reported effect sizes: high intensities above 70 percent MVC/RM produced a clear tendon-stiffness adaptation, while low intensities were near zero.

data.name: PaceModelInfo_LoadAdaptation_Workload_Text
data.value: Warden et al. (2021) frame bone stress injuries not as a consequence of load itself, but as a workload dosing error. For training, this means we do not avoid fast paces; we dose them.

data.name: PaceModelInfo_LoadAdaptationDiagramAlt
data.value: Data-based effect-size diagram of tendon adaptations after mechanical loading from Bohm et al. 2015.

data.name: PaceModelInfo_TrainingScenarios_Title
data.value: Three 12-week training scenarios

data.name: PaceModelInfo_TrainingScenarios_Text
data.value: The following model translates the dosing idea into three training trajectories. Very light loading creates little wear-off but also little adaptive stimulus. Pace-guided training creates regular external load peaks while keeping them close to current tissue capacity; adaptation rises gradually and wear-off decays between sessions. In a heart-rate-guided scenario, external mechanical load can rise as cardiovascular fitness improves even when the session feels internally similar. Load peaks then outpace slower tendon and bone adaptation, and wear-off accumulates.

data.name: PaceModelInfo_TrainingScenariosDiagramAlt
data.value: Didactic 12-week model with three training scenarios, load peaks, adaptation, and wear-off.

data.name: CriticalSpeedBudget_Walk_Title
data.value: Walk

data.name: CriticalSpeedBudget_Walk_Description
data.value: Walking without a prescribed pace.

data.name: CriticalSpeedChart_Title
data.value: Distance vs. pace

data.name: CriticalSpeedChart_Subtitle
data.value: Reference runs used for Critical Speed are highlighted in red.

data.name: CriticalSpeedChart_DistanceAxis
data.value: Distance (km)

data.name: CriticalSpeedChart_PaceAxis
data.value: Pace (min/km)

data.name: CriticalSpeedChart_CriticalPace
data.value: Critical Pace

data.name: CriticalSpeedChart_ContributorLegend
data.value: Used for CS

data.name: CriticalSpeedChart_OtherLegend
data.value: Not used for CS

data.name: CriticalSpeedChart_ContributionStatus
data.value: used for Critical Speed

data.name: CriticalSpeedChart_OtherStatus
data.value: not used for Critical Speed
